dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding to a reported breathing difficulty at 1234 Oak Street.
Hello sir, I'm Maria with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911?
I'm having trouble breathing. It began about two hours ago.
Can you tell me your name and the date today?
My name is Whitney and today is Friday the 20th.
Where are you right now?
I'm in Richmond, I think.
You appear to be in distress. Your airway is patent, but are you able to speak clearly?
Breathing is labored with mild wheezing bilaterally.
Pulse is 94 and strong. Skin is cold and dry.
When did your breathing difficulty begin, and do you have any medical conditions?
It started around 10 PM. I have a dialysis shunt in my left arm. I had treatment on Tuesday. I also have end-stage renal disease and hypertension.
On a 1 to 15 scale, you are at a GCS of 15-alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
You mentioned severe shortness of breath. On a 0 to 10 scale, how would you rate it?
It's a 10. I feel like I can't catch my breath at all.
You mentioned asthma. Has this type of breathing difficulty been related to it before?
Yes, but this feels different. I need CPAP, like before.
Pulse is 94 and regular.
We're going to administer medication to help ease your breathing.
Your respirations are 3 per minute and labored.
I see mild pitting edema in both legs, and no cyanosis. Skin is cold and dry.
Your blood pressure is 203 over 123.
We're going to place CPAP to assist your breathing. It may feel tight, but it will help you breathe easier.
I'm going to attach 4-lead ECG monitoring to establish a baseline rhythm now.
SpO2 is 98% with a pulse oximeter.
We're using a nebulizer treatment of albuterol and ipratropium to open your airways.
Have you previously used CPAP for this issue?
Yes, I've used CPAP in the past for this problem, but never been intubated.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Glucose testing cannot be completed due to the patient's current condition.
I'm going to sit you up to help your breathing, okay?
Current blood pressure is now 204 over 109.
Respirations are now 28 per minute and still labored.
Pulse is 91 and regular.
SpO2 is at 0-possible oximeter issue, trying to resolve.
You'll receive a sublingual nitroglycerin tablet now for possible fluid overload.
The EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
Skin remains cold. Do you feel any numbness or tingling?
No numbness, but the lower legs are swollen.
The EKG now shows possible left atrial enlargement and nonspecific T wave changes.
Blood pressure is now 184 over 102.
Respirations are at 21 and steady.
Pulse is 89 and regular.
SpO2 is 99% after repositioning the oximeter.
I need to establish a 20G IV in your right arm, as the dialysis shunt is in your left.
Current respiration rate is 16 now and showing improvement.
A second dose of 0.4 mg nitroglycerin will be administered now.
Blood pressure is now 185 over 99.
Respirations are now at 16 and continuing to improve.
SpO2 is 98 after adjusting and reapplying the pulse oximeter.
We attempted IV placement but were unsuccessful initially, then succeeded on the second try in your right forearm.
Pulse is 88 now and stable.
SpO2 is now at 100% with current oxygen settings.
The EKG is still in sinus rhythm. No changes observed.
Based on assessments, we are following Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress and possible congestive heart failure.
We are preparing an emergent transport to VCU Health Systems.
Continuous nebulizer treatment is ongoing to support airway dilation and breathing.
The patient is being moved to the ambulance on a stretcher and will be secured for transport.
The patient is now safely on board and secured for transport.
Breathing has improved with CPAP; vitals are stable. No signs of deterioration.
You said you had dialysis on Tuesday. Is this kind of fluid overload something that has happened before?
Yes, but this is worse than anything I have had before.
Skin is still dry and cold with continued bilateral pitting edema. No new changes observed.
Pulse remains stable at 86 and strong.
Respiration is steady at 18 now.
Blood pressure is 198 over 98 now.
SpO2 is 100% and steady on current settings.
EKG shows no acute changes and remains in normal sinus rhythm.
The patient is now secured to the stretcher and inside the ambulance for transport.
A 12-lead ECG has been completed and recorded for review during transport.
All equipment and patient monitoring devices are confirmed functional and stabilized for travel.
You remain fully alert (AOx4) with no signs of confusion or decreased mental status.
Breathing remains at 18 per minute and unlabored.
Blood pressure is stable with a reading of 198 over 98.
Our destination is the Trauma Room at VCU Health Systems for immediate and specialized care.
We've initiated continuous nebulizer treatment that will continue throughout transport.
Responding with lights and sirens for the fastest and safest transport possible.
Unable to collect a phone number due to the patient's distress level, as documented.
Full EPCR and clinical documentation have been handed off to the receiving hospital staff.
The patient is currently in the hospital room and classified as a PUTS (Patient for Urgent Transport by Seniors).
The patient is securely on the stretcher and all transport systems are in place.
VCU is awaiting the patient's arrival and will provide immediate treatment upon arrival.
Pulse remained strong and steady at 86 during transport.
All transport metrics are stable, and the patient is en route without incident.
Blood pressure is consistently documented as 198 over 98 now during transport.
No pain is reported at this time per the patient's statements.
